title: Unreal Engine Games Can Now Run in a Browser VR Tab. This Changes the Distribution Math for Small Studios.
author: Nina Castillo
snippet: Wunder Interactive's toolchain compiles Unreal Engine games to WebAssembly, WebGL, and WebXR. A single codebase now runs on Quest, Vision Pro, Android XR, desktop, and the browser. Here's why that matters more than it sounds.
body:
The hardest part of shipping a VR game in 2026 is not making the game. It is shipping it. Every platform has its own store, its own submission process, its own review cycle, its own SDK quirks, and its own revenue split. A small studio with a single team can spend as much engineering time on per-platform builds as they spent on the gameplay. A company called Wunder Interactive has been quietly building a toolchain that compiles Unreal Engine projects to WebAssembly, WebGL, and WebXR, and the result is that a single Unreal build can now run inside a browser tab on any WebXR-capable headset. That is a meaningful shift in how VR distribution can work, and developers should be paying attention.

What the stack actually does

Wunder Interactive's pipeline compiles the Unreal build target to WebAssembly for the CPU-side game logic, WebGL 2 or WebGPU for the rendering layer, and WebXR for the headset integration. The output is a static bundle of files that can be served from any web server. No app store submission. No binary upload. No review process. The user navigates to a URL in their Quest browser or Vision Pro Safari session, and the game loads into an immersive WebXR context.

If that sounds like what Babylon.js and Three.js already do for web-first VR, the difference is the starting point. Babylon and Three are JavaScript-native engines that were designed for the web from day one. They are great, but they are not Unreal. Wunder's pitch is that developers who already have an Unreal project, with Unreal's lighting, physics, animation, and materials systems, can now ship that project to the web without rewriting any of it. The same scene that runs at 90fps on a PC VR rig can load in a browser tab and run on a Quest 3.

Why a browser tab matters

Distribution is everything in VR. The Quest store is discoverable but curated. SteamVR is open but niche. Vision Pro has its own app store. Android XR is launching with Play Store integration. Every platform takes a cut, and every platform has different promotion rules. The browser cuts all of that out.

A WebXR link can be shared on social media, embedded in a blog post, opened from a QR code, or included in a Discord message. The user does not install anything. They click the link, consent to the immersive session, and they are in the experience. That is the exact same friction profile as a YouTube video, which happens to be the most-watched media format in the world.

For small studios, this is the difference between building a discovery loop and not having one. The current VR store model is winner-take-all. If you rank on the Quest store front page you do well, and if you do not you are invisible. A WebXR link ignores that hierarchy. Every link is equal. Every share is distribution. That is a fundamentally different funnel, and it favors the kinds of small, shareable experiences that have struggled to find oxygen in the app store model.

What it is good for and what it is not

A browser-based Unreal game is not going to be Red Matter 2 or Metro Awakening. Browser runtimes have real performance limits. The current WebAssembly and WebGPU stack cannot hit the same frame budget as a native compiled binary, and memory budgets are tighter because browsers do not give tabs unlimited heap. Wunder's benchmarks show about 70 to 80 percent of native performance, which is good enough for most stylized games and most educational or promotional experiences, but not good enough for the most demanding fidelity targets.

The sweet spot is short-form content. A 10-minute branded VR experience. A product configurator. A museum tour. A playable demo of a larger game. A mini game jam entry. A promotional piece for a film release. These are all things that today require either a full app store submission, which kills the budget math, or a custom web build, which kills the tooling reuse. Wunder's pipeline lets an Unreal studio repurpose their existing assets and logic for web delivery, which makes this category economically viable for the first time.

The competitive picture

Unity has been slowly improving its WebGL export, and WebXR support through the Unity XR stack exists but is rough. Unreal's official web support has historically been worse. If Wunder's tooling delivers on its current demos, it gives Unreal users a better browser export path than Unity's official tooling, which is a strange and interesting reversal. Epic has not announced anything that suggests they will build something similar in-house, which means Wunder has a real window to become the default way Unreal reaches the web.

Godot, which ships native web export as a first-class target, has been the quiet winner of the web-native VR space for a while. Its HTML5 export already handles WebXR, and Meta has been funding that work. Godot 4.6 shipped with improved WebXR support earlier this month. The combination of a free engine and a native web export has made Godot the go-to for anyone who wanted to ship a VR experience through a URL rather than an app store, and Wunder is aiming squarely at that space with a more established engine.

The quiet implication

The most interesting thing about the browser distribution story is what it does to platform power. Meta, Apple, Samsung, and Google each want to be the gatekeeper of VR content for their hardware. Every app store is a toll booth. WebXR is the open highway. If Wunder's tooling or something like it becomes the default way small studios ship Unreal content to VR, then the app store gatekeepers lose leverage over a growing share of the content market.

That shift is not going to happen overnight. But it is the first genuinely credible threat to the app store model in VR since the category began, and it is coming from a tooling layer that most people have not heard of yet. Keep an eye on this one.
